original_hypothesis.title: Presale Whitelist Manipulation - Subject Can Change Merkle Root During Active Presale
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: setWhitelist allows the subject to change the merkle root when supply <= 1. Since the subject buys exactly 1 token during buyCurvesTokenForPresale (supply becomes 1), they can call setWhitelist during an active presale to change the whitelist, excluding already-whitelisted users or adding new users. Existing presale participants who planned to buy more tokens find their proofs invalidated.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: setWhitelist checks supply > 1 to revert, meaning supply=0 or supply=1 is allowed. After buyCurvesTokenForPresale sets supply=1, the subject can modify merkleRoot freely during the presale phase, undermining trust in the whitelist commitment.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setWhitelist: if (supply > 1) revert CurveAlreadyExists(); allows modification when supply == 1 (during active presale)

preconditions: Subject has created a presale via buyCurvesTokenForPresale (supply=1)
Whitelisted users exist who plan to buy

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: supply == 1 after presale creation; setWhitelist only blocks supply > 1
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Subject changes merkle root to exclude certain users or include favored parties after seeing demand
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Subject can manipulate who gets early access, potentially creating unfair advantage for insiders

economic_questions: Can the subject extract economic value by selectively modifying the whitelist?
Can the subject set merkleRoot to bytes32(0) to effectively disable the whitelist?
How does this interact with presalesBuys tracking?
validation_requirements: Verify setWhitelist succeeds when supply=1 during active presale
Test if merkleRoot=0 allows any proof to pass
Confirm whitelist changes take effect immediately

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of initial token purchase (first token is free)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect value from controlling presale access

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During active presale after seeing demand

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of presale access / early pricing advantage

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Presale timing mechanics, merkle root validation edge cases
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test setWhitelist at supply=1 during presale

validation_result.mechanism: Presale whitelist mutability during active presale
validation_result.impact_summary: Subject can change the merkle root via setWhitelist after creating a presale (supply=1), invalidating existing whitelisted users' proofs and potentially replacing them with favored addresses during an active presale window.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The setWhitelist function only checks `supply > 1` to revert, meaning it passes when supply is 0 or 1. After buyCurvesTokenForPresale is called by the subject, supply becomes 1 (since getPrice(0, amount>1) underflows, the subject must buy exactly 1 token). At this point the presale is active (startTime > block.timestamp was enforced in buyCurvesTokenForPresale), yet setWhitelist allows the subject to freely change the merkleRoot. This means the subject can observe which whitelisted users are attempting to buy, then change the whitelist to exclude them or include different addresses. The window of vulnerability exists from presale creation until the first whitelisted user successfully buys (pushing supply to 2+). This is a code defect in the access control logic of setWhitelist — it should also check whether a presale is currently active.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is confirmed by examining three code paths: (1) buyCurvesTokenForPresale sets startTime > block.timestamp and supply becomes 1, (2) setWhitelist allows merkleRoot changes when supply <= 1, and (3) buyCurvesTokenWhitelisted validates the merkle proof against the current (potentially changed) merkleRoot. The code clearly allows the subject to modify whitelist parameters during an active presale.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The guard `supply > 1` allows the function to execute when supply == 1, which is the exact state after buyCurvesTokenForPresale. There is no check for whether a presale is currently active (startTime > 0 && startTime > block.timestamp). This permits the subject to change the merkle root during an active presale, invalidating existing whitelisted users' proofs.

After this function executes, supply becomes 1 (subject buys the first token) and startTime > block.timestamp, establishing an active presale. The supply == 1 state is exactly what allows setWhitelist to succeed.

This function verifies the merkle proof against presalesMeta[curvesTokenSubject].merkleRoot which can be changed by the subject mid-presale. If the subject changes the root, previously valid proofs become invalid, causing verifyMerkle to revert with UnverifiedProof.

calling_sequence: 1. Subject calls buyCurvesTokenForPresale(subject, 1, futureTime, merkleRootA, maxBuy) — sets presale with merkleRootA, supply becomes 1
2. Presale is now active: startTime > block.timestamp, supply == 1
3. Subject calls setWhitelist(merkleRootB) — passes supply > 1 check since supply == 1, merkleRoot changed to merkleRootB
4. Whitelisted user (under merkleRootA) calls buyCurvesTokenWhitelisted with proof for merkleRootA — verifyMerkle fails because root is now merkleRootB
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: buyCurvesTokenForPresale creates the presale and sets supply=1. setWhitelist checks `supply > 1` which is false when supply=1, so it proceeds to update presalesMeta[msg.sender].merkleRoot. buyCurvesTokenWhitelisted then calls verifyMerkle which checks the updated (changed) merkleRoot, causing previously valid proofs to fail.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. The defect is in the logic of setWhitelist's access control.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward: (1) Subject creates presale via buyCurvesTokenForPresale, supply becomes 1. (2) Subject announces whitelist to attract buyers. (3) During active presale (block.timestamp < startTime), subject calls setWhitelist with a new merkle root that excludes original whitelist members or includes different addresses. (4) Original whitelisted users' proofs are now invalid and their buyCurvesTokenWhitelisted calls revert. (5) New whitelisted addresses (controlled by subject or favored parties) can buy at early presale prices. The subject could also set merkleRoot to bytes32(0), which would likely cause all proofs to fail (unless an attacker crafts a proof for a leaf matching the zero root).
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact is a violation of presale integrity. Whitelisted users who were promised early access can be excluded mid-presale. The subject gains the ability to discriminate among buyers after seeing demand, creating an unfair advantage. While the economic impact depends on token demand, this fundamentally breaks the trust model of the whitelist presale mechanism. The vulnerability window exists from presale creation until the first non-subject purchase pushes supply above 1.
recommended_mitigations: Add a check in setWhitelist to prevent modification during active presale: `if (presalesMeta[msg.sender].startTime > 0 && presalesMeta[msg.sender].startTime > block.timestamp) revert PresaleActive();`
Alternatively, change the guard to `if (supply > 0) revert CurveAlreadyExists();` to only allow whitelist setting before any tokens exist
Consider making the merkle root immutable once the presale is created via buyCurvesTokenForPresale
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Curves with factory and fee splitter. 2. As subject, call buyCurvesTokenForPresale with a merkle root for whitelist A and startTime in the future. 3. Verify supply == 1. 4. As subject, call setWhitelist with a different merkle root B. 5. Verify presalesMeta[subject].merkleRoot has changed. 6. As a user from whitelist A, attempt buyCurvesTokenWhitelisted with a valid proof for root A. 7. Confirm the transaction reverts with UnverifiedProof.
uncertainties: The vulnerability window closes once any whitelisted user successfully buys (supply becomes 2+), limiting the attack window but not eliminating it
If the subject buys more than 1 token initially (which would require supply>0 already), the window might differ, but getPrice(0, amount>1) underflows so only amount=1 is possible at creation
additional_testing_needed: Test what happens when merkleRoot is set to bytes32(0) — does MerkleProof.verify accept any proof or reject all proofs?
Test the exact timing: can the subject change whitelist in the same block as presale creation?
Test if subject can also modify startTime or maxBuy through other means during active presale
